---
title: "Measles Susceptibility in Glasgow Primary School Children"
author: "DAS Group 15"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r}
# load packages
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

```{r}
dat <- read.csv("DAProject15.csv")

# create proportion column (Y susceptible out of N total)
dat$prop <- dat$Y / dat$N

# centre year at 2004 - makes the intercept easier to interpret
# (i.e. intercept = log-odds at 2004, not at year 0 which makes no sense)
dat$yr_c <- dat$Year - 2004

# indicator for post-retraction period
dat$post <- ifelse(dat$Year >= 2004, 1, 0)
```

# Introduction

The measles, mumps and rubella (MMR) vaccine has been an important part
of childhood immunisation programmes in the UK since the late 1980s. In
1998, Wakefield published a paper claiming a link between the MMR
vaccine and autism. Although the study was later found to be fraudulent
and was fully retracted in 2010, the media coverage it received caused
vaccination rates to drop significantly and fall to around 80% in some
parts of the UK by 2003. This eventually contributed to a large measles
outbreak in 2013.

A partial retraction of the paper occurred in 2004, which helped public
perception of the vaccine. This report uses data from the Scottish
Childhood Immunisation Record System (SCIRS) to investigate whether
measles susceptibility among primary school children in Glasgow changed
over the period 1998--2012, and whether any change can be linked to the
2004 partial retraction of the paper.

The data cover 133 intermediate zones (IZs) in Glasgow, which are small
geographic units of roughly 4,000 residents each. For each IZ and year,
we have the number of primary school children susceptible to measles
(`Y`) and the total number of primary school children (`N`).

The two questions we aim to answer are:

1.  Did measles susceptibility in Glasgow change following the Wakefield
    retraction?
2.  Did this change occur specifically around 2004?

# Exploratory Data Analysis

```{r}
# summarise by year - want mean proportion, sd, min, max per year
year_sum <- dat %>%
  group_by(Year) %>%
  summarise(
    n_iz     = n(),
    mean_p   = round(mean(prop), 4),
    sd_p     = round(sd(prop), 4),
    min_p    = round(min(prop), 4),
    max_p    = round(max(prop), 4)
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics for the proportion of primary school children susceptible to measles, by year."

kable(year_sum,
      col.names = c("Year", "IZs", "Mean", "SD", "Min", "Max"),
      booktabs  = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "hold_position", font_size = 9) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "Proportion susceptible" = 4))
```

@tbl-summary shows how the mean proportion of susceptible children
changed over time. There is an increase from 1998 through to around
2003, followed by a decline from 2004 onwards, which lines up with the
timing of the Wakefield retraction.

```{r}
#| label: fig-trend
#| fig-cap: "Mean proportion susceptible per year across all 133 Glasgow IZs. Error bars show +/- 1 standard error. The dashed line marks 2004."
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| fig-align: center

ggplot(year_sum, aes(x = Year, y = mean_p)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(colour = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_p - sd_p/sqrt(n_iz),
                    ymax = mean_p + sd_p/sqrt(n_iz)),
                width = 0.3, colour = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2004, linetype = "dashed", colour = "firebrick") +
  annotate("text", x = 2004.2, y = max(year_sum$mean_p) * 0.99,
           label = "2004 retraction", hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Mean proportion susceptible") +
  theme_minimal()
```

@fig-trend shows the overall pattern of susceptibility rising from 1998,
peaks around 2003, then falls after 2004. This is consistent with the
drop in vaccination rates following the 1998 paper, and a recovery in
uptake after the retraction.

```{r}
#| label: fig-box
#| fig-cap: "IZ-level proportion susceptible by year. Each box summarises the 133 IZs for that year. Dashed line marks 2004."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| fig-align: center

ggplot(dat, aes(x = factor(Year), y = prop)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightsteelblue", outlier.size = 0.7, outlier.alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 6.5,   # 2004 is the 7th year (1998,99,00,01,02,03,2004)
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Proportion susceptible") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

@fig-box shows the distribution across IZs for each year. There is
moderate variation between IZs, which makes sense given that different
areas will have different vaccination uptake.

# Formal Analysis

## Model

Since our outcome is a count of susceptible children out of a total
(i.e., Y out of N), the model proposed is a binomial GLM with a logit
link, which is a logistic regression model but for proportional count
data rather than individual 0/1 outcomes.

We want to test whether the trend in susceptibility changed at 2004, so
we fit a model with an interaction term between year and the post-2004
indicator:

$$
\log\left(\frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Year}_i^* + \beta_2 \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{post}] + \beta_3 \cdot (\text{Year}_i^* \times \mathbb{1}[\text{post}])
$$

where $\text{Year}^*$ is the year centred at 2004, and
$\mathbb{1}[\text{post}]$ equals 1 for 2004 and later. The interaction
$\beta_3$ captures whether the slope differs before vs after 2004.
